verses.text: Solomon takes thirteen years to build his own house — longer than the temple's seven. The detail is quietly telling: the king's palace got more years than God's house. We should weigh how the time and care we give our own comfort compares with what we give to God.

The porch of pillars, the hall of judgment where Solomon's throne stood, and the royal residences are all described. The king built fitting places to rule and to judge. Earthly authority needs its ordered courts — yet all of it stands in the shadow of the house built for God.

Costly, hewn stones, sawn to measure, form the foundations and the great court, with cedar above. The work is expensive and exact throughout. Excellence and cost mark everything Solomon built; what is worth doing, for king or for God, is worth doing well.

Solomon brings Hiram from Tyre, a craftsman 'filled with wisdom, and understanding, and cunning to work all works in brass.' His skill, like Bezaleel's, was a gift of God. The LORD equips particular people with particular gifts for the building of His house.

Solomon brings Hiram from Tyre, a craftsman 'filled with wisdom, and understanding, and cunning to work all works in brass.' His skill, like Bezaleel's, was a gift of God. The LORD equips particular people with particular gifts for the building of His house.

Hiram casts two great bronze pillars for the temple porch, crowned with ornate capitals of network and pomegranates. They stand as grand, beautiful sentinels at the door of God's house. Even the entrance proclaimed His majesty to all who came to worship.

The pillars are set up and named — Jachin, 'He shall establish,' and Boaz, 'In Him is strength.' Their very names preach to every worshiper who passes between them. Our standing and our strength are not our own; they rest wholly in the God we come to worship.

Hiram casts the molten sea, a vast basin holding thousands of baths, resting on twelve oxen facing out to the four directions. Here the priests washed before serving. Drawing near to God requires cleansing, and the provision for it is abundant and ready.

Ten bronze bases are made, mounted on wheels, ornately carved with lions, oxen, and cherubim. They are wrought with great skill and beauty. Even the movable furniture of God's house was made glorious; nothing about His worship was treated as merely functional.

Ten bronze bases are made, mounted on wheels, ornately carved with lions, oxen, and cherubim. They are wrought with great skill and beauty. Even the movable furniture of God's house was made glorious; nothing about His worship was treated as merely functional.

The bases are described in careful detail — their borders, panels, axles, and wheels like a chariot's. The Spirit-given craftsman spared no precision. Faithful workmanship attends to what no casual eye will notice, because the work is done unto God.

The bases are described in careful detail — their borders, panels, axles, and wheels like a chariot's. The Spirit-given craftsman spared no precision. Faithful workmanship attends to what no casual eye will notice, because the work is done unto God.

The bases are described in careful detail — their borders, panels, axles, and wheels like a chariot's. The Spirit-given craftsman spared no precision. Faithful workmanship attends to what no casual eye will notice, because the work is done unto God.

All ten bases are cast of one measure, one size, one form — uniform and exact. The skill produced order and harmony, not chance variety. God's house reflects His own character: a place of order and consistency, fashioned by wisdom from above.

Ten lavers rest on the bases, five on each side, with the great sea by the corner, all for washing. The house is well supplied with the means of cleansing. There is no lack of provision for purity in the service of God; He makes ample way for His own to be clean.

Ten lavers rest on the bases, five on each side, with the great sea by the corner, all for washing. The house is well supplied with the means of cleansing. There is no lack of provision for purity in the service of God; He makes ample way for His own to be clean.

Hiram completes the pots, shovels, basins, and all the temple's bronze work for Solomon. Every needed vessel is supplied. The worship of God requires many ordinary instruments, and the faithful craftsman makes them all — the humble tools as carefully as the grand pillars.

So much bronze is used that 'the weight... was not found out,' beyond all reckoning. The abundance overflows counting. When hearts give wholly to God's house, the supply runs past measure; lavish devotion does not stop to weigh its gifts.

Finally the golden vessels — the altar, the table of shewbread, the lampstands, the bowls — are made, and David's dedicated treasures are brought in. The work is complete, joining the father's gifts to the son's. God's house is built across generations, each adding to the last.
